previous_statement: (Pointedness forces exit from cone) Since V^+ is pointed, we cannot have both D ∈ V^+ and -D ∈ V^+ (this would imply D = 0). Since x lies in the relative interior of F and D ∈ span(F), for sufficiently small |t| we have x + tD ∈ F ⊆ V^+. But the line cannot remain in V^+ in both directions indefinitely. Define t^+ = sup{t > 0 : x + tD ∈ V^+} and t^- = sup{t > 0 : x - tD ∈ V^+}. At least one of t^+, t^- is finite.
new_statement: (Algebraic relative interior of F) The face F = face_{V^+}(x) is by definition the smallest face containing x in its relative interior. For any D ∈ span(F), write D = w_1 - w_2 with w_1, w_2 ∈ F. By definition of F, there exist λ_1, λ_2 > 0 with x - λ_i w_i ∈ V^+. For 0 < t < min(λ_1, λ_2): x + tD = (x - tw_2) + tw_1 ∈ V^+ where x - tw_2 = (1 - t/λ_2)x + (t/λ_2)(x - λ_2 w_2) ∈ V^+ by convexity. Same for x - tD. Hence x ± εD ∈ F ⊆ V^+ for small ε > 0.
